title: 2025/10/16 - JU [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2025-10-16 - Justice Committee - Antisocial Road Use Legislation Amendment Bill [Part 2]
topic: Antisocial Road Use Legislation Amendment Bill
description: Thursday 16 October 2025 | Justice Committee Antisocial Road Use Legislation Amendment Bill Submitters: New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa - Paul Rishworth KC - Chris Macklin New Zealand Automobile Association - Terry Collins, Principal Policy Advisor - Dylan Thomsen, Communications, Research and Road Safety Manager Horowhenua District Council - Mayor Bernie Wanden Federated Farmers of NZ Inc - Karl Dean, Rural Policing spokesperson - Jade McCormick, Senior Policy Adviser Quieter and Safer Roads Task Force, Lyttelton Harbour - Scott Adams, Secretary - Dave Dunlay, Spokesman Community Law Centres Aotearoa - Karen Raewyn Hodgson - Andy Olgivie, Solicitor, Canterbury Community Law - Angela Smith, Legal Services Manager, Waikato Community Law The Law Association of New Zealand - Samira Taghavi, Barrister and member of the TLANZ Criminal Law Committee Danika Marshall
descriptionSummary: Antisocial Road Use Legislation Amendment Bill. New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa. Paul Rishworth KC

We've got 10 minutes, right? Thanks very much. And so the first point is mine. Second to, uh, from, from Chris.

Uh, I'm gonna talk about the power to compel information from owners, uh, as to who was driving. And that's on page four of our written submission. And it starts at paragraph 5.1, but I'll, I'll just explain it in, in my own words and, and not follow the, uh, text.

But that, that's where I'm, uh, that that's where it all is in the submission. The point is that in this bill, there was quite understandably gonna be a legal power to compel a registered owner of a vehicle to provide identifying information as to who was driving it on some occasion. And is the offense then of failing to identify?

And that's, that's what makes it a compulsion to identify. And the issue that we address in the submission is what's to be done about the right against self-incrimination, which New Zealand law recognizes in the Evidence Act and the Bill of Rights. Uh, to be clear, that right isn't generally impaired when you simply ask an owner to identify the driver.

That isn't the problem. The point is that in some cases, the phenomenon of of, of identifying the driver might implicate the owner in criminal activity and would therefore be something that he has a right not to do in terms of the right against self-incrimination.

So that's a possibility inherent in all sorts of statutes that require information to be provided compulsorily. And the society's point is simply that in other contexts, the law is made clear that there is a right against self-incrimination, which operates as an exception to the compulsory power to provide information.

So the law society says that the same sort of wording ought to be in this bill. And we've set out two other statutes, which have examples of the point more generally. That's the Search and Surveillance Act and the anti-Money laundering and Counter Financing of Terrorism Act. So that's the problem. Uh, the Crown Law opinion about why the bill is consistent with the Bill of Rights recognizes that essential problem.

But it says that well and practice the courts can give discre, uh, can give relief against a person who incriminates themselves by declaring the evidence inadmissible. And, and we say to that, that it's, it's not conceptually right to rely upon that post facto discretion. The law ought to not to be enacted in a form that contemplates the possibility of right speeches and then simply requires the person to rely upon a discretion.

It ought this bill, like the other examples we've given to positively state the existence of the right against self-incrimination. And that would have the advantage that a person would know about it at the time they're asked to, uh, to give the information about who was driving. So that's a potted summary of the point in our submission.

And so we would just recommend that the comm, that the committee looks carefully at that and considers bringing this into line with the other examples that we've given. So I'll pass to Chris for the second two points. Uh, Kia Paul and Committee.

Uh, I have in front of me, you probably have in front of you. The point, um, that we're trying to get at here is that the definition at the moment is unworkable broad. That's clearly intended to cut off any, what shall I say, like innocent intermediaries negating a convoy.

So by slotting in the odd Honda Jazz, driven by an innocent person off to do the groceries, um, we're not gonna undermine the convoy, but the way it's been drafted then cast the net more widely than that and, and effectively removes the intent provisions that I think need to underpin an intimidating gathering of vehicles.

And so the society has said that you need to be together. Yeah. You Got, So, um, from the comments there, I'm, I'm picking up that we're, we're singing to the choir on that one. And this is where provision is made for forfeiture and relief from it via appeal, so that an innocent third party who buys a vehicle that's been used in a convoy, um, has a right to try and keep the vehicle even though there may have been some forfeiture audit in respect of its use in the convoy and the way that it's been drafted, places this in the court's civil jurisdiction, whereas the closest analog here is actually the criminal forfeiture regime as an instrument of crime.

Our district, unfortunately, has experienced the serious consequences of antisocial road use firsthand. And one year later, in May, 2025, a so-called anniversary tomboy brought more than a thousand people together under the same pretext. Despite significant police preparation, the gathering escalated into violence, fireworks, physical aggression, and a vehicle where were used against officers.

Uh, by the end of the night, seven people, including two police officers were injured and a number were arrested. Situations, um, situations. The proposed increases in infringement penalties and the introduction of a new frightening or intimidating convoy offense.
